question: Can you share an experience where an external contributor reacted defensively to feedback from your agent on their pull request? How did you de-escalate the situation, and what did you learn about designing tools that preserve human relationships while still automating tasks?

conversation.message:
Let’s talk about another aspect of collaboration. I’m curious about how you handle situations where emotions or defensiveness come into play. For example, in your open-source work with Auto-Dev-Reviewer, you collaborated with developers outside your direct team, which can sometimes lead to differing opinions or even tension. 

Can you share an experience where an external contributor reacted defensively to feedback from your agent on their pull request? How did you de-escalate the situation, and what did you learn about designing tools that preserve human relationships while still automating tasks?

Oh man, yeah—developers can be *very* attached to their code. There was this one PR where the agent flagged a bunch of style violations and even suggested some refactoring. The contributor came back pretty hot, like, "This is just nitpicking, my code works fine."

First, I made sure to acknowledge their work upfront—like, "Hey, I see you put a lot of effort into this, and it’s solid." Then I pointed them to the project’s style guide (which the agent had linked) and explained that the feedback wasn’t personal, just about keeping the codebase consistent. I also tweaked the agent’s tone to be less robotic in its comments, adding things like "Consider this suggestion" instead of "Fix this."

The contributor cooled down after that, and they even ended up making most of the changes. It taught me that automation can’t replace human nuance—you’ve got to design these tools to *support* collaboration, not replace it. Now the agent’s prompts include more context and softer language by default.
